findings.description: reachable() never checks visited before enqueuing neighbors, so on a cyclic graph (which the docstring says it supports) the BFS queue never drains and it loops forever.
Even on an acyclic graph, nodes are re-enqueued for every incoming edge with no visited guard, causing redundant/exponential reprocessing on diamond-shaped DAGs.
The start node is never added to seen, so reachable() omits start even when it is reachable from itself via a cycle, contradicting typical reachability semantics.
Indexing the defaultdict via self._edges[node] for a node with no outgoing edges mutates the dict by inserting an empty-list entry during a read-only traversal.
count_paths() recurses without memoization, giving exponential time on dense DAGs where many sub-paths are recomputed.
count_paths() has no cycle guard, so a cyclic graph (violating the DAG assumption) causes infinite recursion / RecursionError rather than a defined error.
Deep but acyclic chains can exceed Python's default recursion limit, raising RecursionError instead of returning a count.
self._edges[src] on the defaultdict inserts an empty-list key for leaf nodes during counting, mutating graph state as a side effect of a query.
neighbors() indexes the defaultdict, so querying neighbors of an unknown node silently creates an empty-list entry and grows the node-less edge map.
reachable() does not validate that start exists in the graph; an unknown start returns an empty set indistinguishable from a real isolated node.
add_edge appends duplicates to the adjacency list, so a repeated edge inflates count_paths and topological indegree counts.
Iterable is imported but never used.
